name: Inflammatory acne
dek: The painful, red, often pustular lesions that distinguish inflammatory acne from comedonal. The protocol shifts from retinoid-led to retinoid-plus-bactericidal, with a careful eye on antibiotic resistance — the single under-discussed risk in the category.
oneliner: Papules, pustules, nodules. BPO + retinoid + sometimes oral.
bottom: If you have not improved on adapalene + BPO at month 4, see a clinician. Looping on OTC for a year leaves scars that no protocol can undo.

triggers.n: The lesion that hurts when you press it. Inflammation is the dominant feature, not congestion.
The classic 'pimple.' BPO and topical retinoid is the workhorse combination.
If present, escalate to a dermatologist. OTC routine alone is insufficient and risks scarring.
The shadow that remains for months after the lesion clears. Treat in parallel — see PIH guide.

ingredients.name: Benzoyl peroxide 2.5%
ingredients.evidence: First-line. Prevents C. acnes resistance. Pair with adapalene; spot-treat at first.

ingredients.name: Adapalene 0.1%
ingredients.evidence: OTC. Reduces both inflammatory and comedonal lesions. 12-week ramp.

ingredients.name: Clindamycin 1% topical
ingredients.evidence: Effective; never use as monotherapy. Always pair with BPO to prevent resistance.

ingredients.name: Azelaic 15%
ingredients.evidence: Pregnancy-safe; useful in adult inflammatory cases. Slower than retinoids.

ingredients.name: Oral isotretinoin (clinic)
ingredients.evidence: Reference treatment for moderate-severe disease. Requires monitoring; remarkable endpoints.

ingredients.name: Spironolactone (clinic)
ingredients.evidence: First-line for adult female hormonal inflammatory acne. Not for male patients.

phases.b: BPO 2.5% wash 3× per week. Cleanser, ceramide moisturiser, SPF. No actives stacked yet.
Adapalene 0.1% PM, alternate nights. BPO 2.5% spot AM as needed. Watch for resistance signs.
Adapalene + BPO 2.5% combination (Epiduo) is the simplest single-product version of the gold standard.
If inadequate response: dermatologist for oral spironolactone, doxycycline, or isotretinoin. Do not loop on OTC alone.
